primaryStudies.citation: Gudmundsson GH, Agerberth B, Odeberg J, et al. The human gene FALL39 and processing of the cathelin precursor to the antibacterial peptide LL-37 in granulocytes. Eur J Biochem. 1996;238(2):325-332.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: First characterization of human cathelicidin LL-37; identified processing from hCAP18 precursor; established antimicrobial activity

primaryStudies.citation: Agerberth B, Gunne H, Odeberg J, et al. FALL-39, a putative human peptide antibiotic, is cysteine-free and expressed in bone marrow and testis. Proc Natl Acad Sci USA. 1995;92(1):195-199.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Initial discovery of the human cathelicidin gene; identified FALL-39 (later renamed LL-37); cysteine-free structure unique among antimicrobial peptides

primaryStudies.citation: Zanetti M, Gennaro R, Romeo D. Cathelicidins: a novel protein family with a common proregion and a variable C-terminal antimicrobial domain. FEBS Lett. 1995;374(1):1-5.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Established cathelicidin as a protein family; defined structural organization with cathelin-like proregion and C-terminal antimicrobial domain

primaryStudies.citation: Turner J, Cho Y, Dinh NN, et al. Activities of LL-37, a cathelin-associated antimicrobial peptide of human neutrophils. Antimicrob Agents Chemother. 1998;42(9):2206-2214.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria; salt-sensitive activity; membrane disruption mechanism

primaryStudies.citation: Oren Z, Lerman JC, Gudmundsson GH, et al. Structure and organization of the human antimicrobial peptide LL-37 in phospholipid membranes: relevance to the molecular basis for its non-cell-selective activity. Biochem J. 1999;341(Pt 3):501-513.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Alpha-helical structure in membrane environment; mechanism of membrane disruption; non-selective activity explained by amphipathic helix

primaryStudies.citation: Heilborn JD, Nilsson MF, Kratz G, et al. The cathelicidin anti-microbial peptide LL-37 is involved in re-epithelialization of human skin wounds and is lacking in chronic ulcer epithelium. J Invest Dermatol. 2003;120(3):379-389.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 upregulated in acute wounds but absent in chronic non-healing ulcers; essential for wound healing and re-epithelialization

primaryStudies.citation: Frohm M, Agerberth B, Ahangari G, et al. The expression of the gene coding for the antibacterial peptide LL-37 is induced in human keratinocytes during inflammatory disorders. J Biol Chem. 1997;272(24):15258-15263.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 induced during inflammation; keratinocytes produce LL-37 in psoriasis but not normal skin

primaryStudies.citation: Nizet V, Ohtake T, Lauth X, et al. Innate antimicrobial peptide protects the skin from invasive bacterial infection. Nature. 2001;414(6862):454-457.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Cathelicidin-deficient mice susceptible to Group A Streptococcus skin infection; first in vivo proof of essential antimicrobial role

primaryStudies.citation: Murakami M, Lopez-Garcia B, Braff M, et al. Postsecretory processing generates multiple cathelicidins for enhanced topical antimicrobial defense. J Immunol. 2004;172(5):3070-3077.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 processed to smaller fragments with enhanced antimicrobial activity; kallikrein proteases involved; skin-specific processing

primaryStudies.citation: Lande R, Gregorio J, Facchinetti V, et al. Plasmacytoid dendritic cells sense self-DNA coupled with antimicrobial peptide. Nature. 2007;449(7162):564-569.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 binds self-DNA and triggers TLR9 activation in plasmacytoid dendritic cells; mechanism linking LL-37 to psoriasis autoimmunity

primaryStudies.citation: Dorschner RA, Pestonjamasp VK, Tamakuwala S, et al. Cutaneous injury induces the release of cathelicidin anti-microbial peptides active against group A Streptococcus. J Invest Dermatol. 2001;117(1):91-97.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 released into wound fluid at antimicrobially active concentrations; protects against wound infection

primaryStudies.citation: Chromek M, Slamova Z, Bergman P, et al. The antimicrobial peptide cathelicidin protects the urinary tract against invasive bacterial infection. Nat Med. 2006;12(6):636-641.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 essential for urinary tract defense; deficiency linked to recurrent UTIs; produced by kidney epithelial cells

primaryStudies.citation: Yamasaki K, Di Nardo A, Bardan A, et al. Increased serine protease activity and cathelicidin promotes skin inflammation in rosacea. Nat Med. 2007;13(8):975-980.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Abnormal cathelicidin processing produces inflammatory fragments in rosacea; kallikrein 5 overactivity; therapeutic target identification

primaryStudies.citation: Scott MG, Davidson DJ, Gold MR, et al. The human antimicrobial peptide LL-37 is a multifunctional modulator of innate immune responses. J Immunol. 2002;169(7):3883-3891.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 modulates dendritic cell differentiation; chemotactic for neutrophils, monocytes, T cells; immunomodulatory beyond direct antimicrobial

primaryStudies.citation: Bowdish DM, Davidson DJ, Hancock RE. A re-evaluation of the role of host defence peptides in mammalian immunity. Curr Protein Pept Sci. 2005;6(1):35-51.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Comprehensive review of immunomodulatory functions; paradigm shift from antimicrobial to host defense peptide concept

primaryStudies.citation: Ramos R, Silva JP, Rodrigues AC, et al. Wound healing activity of the human antimicrobial peptide LL37. Peptides. 2011;32(7):1469-1476.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 promotes keratinocyte and fibroblast migration; accelerates wound closure in vivo; EGFR transactivation mechanism

primaryStudies.citation: Koczulla R, von Degenfeld G, Kupatt C, et al. An angiogenic role for the human peptide antibiotic LL-37/hCAP-18. J Clin Invest. 2003;111(11):1665-1672.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 promotes angiogenesis via FPRL1 receptor; stimulates endothelial cell proliferation; therapeutic potential in ischemic diseases

primaryStudies.citation: Lipsky BA, Holroyd KJ, Zasloff M. Topical versus systemic antimicrobial therapy for treating mildly infected diabetic foot ulcers: a randomized, controlled, double-blinded, multicenter trial of pexiganan cream. Clin Infect Dis. 2008;47(12):1537-1545.
primaryStudies.population: 835 patients with infected diabetic foot ulcers
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Pexiganan (magainin analog) non-inferior to ofloxacin for diabetic foot infections; proof of concept for antimicrobial peptide therapeutics

primaryStudies.citation: Nell MJ, Tjabringa GS, Wafelman AR, et al. Development of novel LL-37 derived antimicrobial peptides with LPS and LTA neutralizing and antimicrobial activities for therapeutic application. Peptides. 2006;27(4):649-660.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: LL-37 derivatives (P60.4Ac, P60) with enhanced activity and stability; neutralize endotoxin; reduced cytotoxicity; therapeutic candidates

primaryStudies.citation: Groenink J, Walgreen-Weterings E, van 't Hof W, et al. Cationic amphipathic peptides, derived from bovine and human lactoferrins, with antimicrobial activity against oral pathogens. FEMS Microbiol Lett. 1999;179(2):217-222.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Cationic peptides effective against oral bacteria including Streptococcus mutans; potential for dental applications

primaryStudies.citation: Larrick JW, Hirata M, Balint RF, et al. Human CAP18: a novel antimicrobial lipopolysaccharide-binding protein. Infect Immun. 1995;63(4):1291-1297.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: hCAP18 binds and neutralizes LPS (endotoxin); potential for sepsis therapy; anti-inflammatory beyond antimicrobial

primaryStudies.citation: Johansson J, Gudmundsson GH, Rottenberg ME, et al. Conformation-dependent antibacterial activity of the naturally occurring human peptide LL-37. J Biol Chem. 1998;273(6):3718-3724.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Alpha-helical structure required for antibacterial activity; central helix region essential; structure-activity relationship established

primaryStudies.citation: Liu PT, Stenger S, Li H, et al. Toll-like receptor triggering of a vitamin D-mediated human antimicrobial response. Science. 2006;311(5768):1770-1773.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Vitamin D induces LL-37 expression in macrophages; TLR activation triggers vitamin D pathway; explains vitamin D role in tuberculosis immunity

primaryStudies.citation: Wang G, Mishra B, Lau K, et al. Antimicrobial peptides in 2014. Pharmaceuticals (Basel). 2015;8(1):123-150.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Comprehensive review of antimicrobial peptide field including LL-37; clinical development status; therapeutic strategies

primaryStudies.citation: Fabisiak A, Murawska N, Fichna J. LL-37: Cathelicidin-related antimicrobial peptide with pleiotropic activity. Pharmacol Rep. 2016;68(4):802-808.
primaryStudies.keyFindings: Comprehensive review of LL-37 functions: antimicrobial, immunomodulatory, wound healing, angiogenesis, anticancer

trials.title: LL-37 for Treatment of Venous Leg Ulcers
trials.notes: Safety and efficacy of topical LL-37 in chronic venous leg ulcers

trials.title: LL-37 in Melanoma
trials.notes: Intratumoral LL-37 for malignant melanoma immunotherapy

trials.title: Omiganan for Rosacea
trials.notes: Related antimicrobial peptide therapeutic for LL-37-associated skin condition

evidenceSummary.regulatoryStatus: Not approved as therapeutic; investigational; natural human peptide
evidenceSummary.keyLimitation: Native peptide with extensive mechanistic understanding but challenges in stability, delivery, and cost for therapeutic development

discoveryHistory.discoveredBy: Birgitta Agerberth, Gudmundur H. Gudmundsson, and colleagues
discoveryHistory.renameReason: Named for 37 amino acids starting with two leucines (LL)
